Title: Mega project, limited public profile
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Little-known Malaysian firm Helios Photovoltaic Sdn Bhd plans to develop a RM5.5 billion project in Kelantan and list on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange, raising eyebrows over the scale of the proposal relative to its limited public profile.
The company is targeting €33 million proceed from its initial public offering (IPO) and has appointed SMC Investmentbank AG as its listing bank, with the offering targeted for next month.
Details on the company are scarce with no clear track record of large-scale developments or major projects typically associated with investments of this magnitude.
However, at a press conference yesterday, the company outlined three solar projects in Sarawak that were awarded from the state government.
Helios also claimed to have a floating solar project in Sembawang, Singapore and is in talks with Cambodia's authorities for solar industrial parks there.
Domestically, the company is in discussions with related parties to include a solar and green industrial park at Kota Kinabalu International Industrial Park in Sabah.
Top Helios executives said it plans to build a solar park as well as a wildlife safari conservation and resort project on about 1,214 hectares in northern Kelantan.
The solar park will feature a 1,000-megawatt-peak solar photovoltaic plant paired with a 1,600-megawatt-hour battery energy storage system.
Helios chairman Datuk Seri Ibrahim Muhammad said the project is being developed under the government's Corporate Renewable Energy Supply Scheme.
It is designed as a scalable platform that integrates renewable energy generation with opportunities for investment participation, he added.
"Through our proprietary zero net energy lots concept, corporate and investors can participate starting from as low as one megawatt without the need to build or operate the infrastructure themselves," he told a press conference here today.
Ibrahim added that Helios is actively seeking partnerships with international conservation and ecotourism organisations to further strengthen the initiative.
This includes developing ecotourism resorts aimed at promoting sustainable interactions with nature.
Ibrahim added that the effort includes developing ecotourism resorts aimed at fostering sustainable interactions with nature.
It is unclear whether the project has secured firm regulatory approvals, land ownership clarity or committed financing partners, all key prerequisites for developments of such scale.
However, Helios chief operating officer Datuk Dr Zuraidi Ishak claimed that it is holding a series of discussions with various potential international investors, mostly from China and Europe.
"The discussions are still ongoing and no decisions have been made yet, but I expect them to be finalised by the middle of the year," he added.
On the IPO, Ibrahim said the listing proceeds will be used to support the development and expansion of solar projects across Europe and Asia, including Germany, Malaysia, Singapore and Cambodia.
When asked why the German market was chosen for the listing, Zuraidi said the decision was driven by Helios' expansion plans.
He added that the company intends to channel the IPO proceeds into projects in Germany, Uzbekistan and Kazakhstan, while continuing its operations in Singapore, Cambodia and Malaysia.
"Our focus is more overseas, more towards the European Union. In addition to Germany, we are also looking at eastern European countries such as Slovakia and Bulgaria. So I think that's the answer why we are focusing there and not the local bourse," he said.
Helios may consider acquiring local companies and participating in Bursa Malaysia, he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC seeking UK evidence in probe into international law firm
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) is working to obtain evidence from the United Kingdom as part of its probe into international law firm White & Case over its alleged links to the 1Malaysia Development Bhd (1MDB) scandal.
At the heart of the probe are allegations that the firm was involved in preparing documents containing false or misleading information, as well as payment instructions that purportedly facilitated the transfer of 1MDB-linked funds to unauthorised parties.
MACC Chief Commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki told the
New Straits Times
that investigators had identified elements of offences under Malaysian law, including provisions under the MACC Act 2009 and the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001.
Having completed the domestic leg of the investigation, he said the case now hinged on securing key documentary and testimonial evidence from abroad before any final enforcement decision could be made.
The firm has been linked to the alleged misappropriation of US$1 billion in 1MDB funds, including the diversion of about US$700 million to an offshore entity beyond 1MDB's control.
"The investigation has uncovered elements of wrongdoing, but further evidence from overseas is required to strengthen the case," Azam said.
MACC is pursuing evidence through formal channels, including the Attorney-General's Chambers (A-GC) and Mutual Legal Assistance requests.
The next phase is expected to focus heavily on cooperation with UK authorities, where White & Case's offices and relevant financial trails are located.
Investigators are also scrutinising payments made to the law firm that are believed to be directly linked to the alleged misappropriation of 1MDB assets, as well as its role in facilitating joint venture arrangements.
The probe aligned with earlier reports that Malaysian authorities were examining whether the firm had a role in transactions tied to the controversial 1MDB-PetroSaudi joint venture.
UK-based news portal The Observer highlighted concerns over a US$700 million transfer linked to the venture, which was allegedly routed to an entity outside 1MDB's control, raising questions over due diligence and the role of professional advisers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Give up PPR units for those more deserving, says former tenant
Article:
GEORGE TOWN:  After 13 years in a People's Housing Programme (PPR) unit, Nazrimah Jamal Abidin has turned a new chapter in her life — moving out of the low-cost unit and into her own home.
Nazrimah, 51, who lived at the Taman Manggis PPR, said her journey reflected  perseverance and the importance of fair access to public housing.
"I moved into the PPR when my household income was about RM1,200. My husband earned  a daily wage and we were struggling," she said
Nazrimah said she left the PPR  in 2019 after securing a rent-to-own home under a state  scheme, to be paid over 20 years.
"I am very grateful to the state government for giving me this opportunity. Life was not easy, but now I can stand on my own."
She  now  lives with her children, one studying at Universiti Teknologi Mara in Shah Alam.
Her story comes amid  eviction notices issued to 18 long-term occupants of the  Taman Manggis PPR, some of whom have  stayed  for up to 20 years.
Nazrimah said while PPRs  are  for the truly needy,  some residents may outgrow their eligibility yet remain.
She also claimed that some residents may underreport household income, despite having working children.
"If they can afford it, they should move out. Others more deserving are  waiting."
Nazrimah added that extended stays despite improved circumstances could be seen as taking advantage of the system.
On the state government's decision to enforce evictions, she said: "The authorities must be firm. I support the move because it gives others in need a chance to have a place to stay."
Nazrimah said leaving the PPR environment was a significant emotional milestone for her.
"I feel relieved and thankful. It's not easy to come out of poverty, but with effort and the right support, it is possible," she said.
She hopes her experience will encourage others to be transparent about their financial situation and to make way for those in genuine need.
"Life was never easy for me, but I am grateful for what I have today," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump suspends Iran bombing for two weeks, after apocalyptic threats
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Tuesday he was suspending bombing of Iran for two weeks but that Teheran must reopen the key Strait of Hormuz, barely an hour before his apocalyptic deadline to destroy the country was set to expire.
After more than five weeks of blistering attacks on Iran by the United States and Israel, Trump said he had accepted a proposal mediated by Pakistan to extend his deadline but he again pushed on the Strait of Hormuz, the waterway vital for the world's oil.
Trump said he had spoke to Pakistan's leaders who "requested that I hold off the destructive force being sent tonight to Iran."
"And subject to the Islamic Republic of Iran agreeing to the COMPLETE, IMMEDIATE, and SAFE OPENING of the Strait of Hormuz, I agree to suspend the bombing and attack of Iran for a period of two weeks," Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform.
"The reason for doing so is that we have already met and exceeded all Military objectives, and are very far along with a definitive Agreement concerning Longterm PEACE with Iran, and PEACE in the Middle East," Trump wrote.
He said that Iran had sent a 10-point plan to the United States that he called "workable" for negotiations.
The price of oil quickly fell sharply on Trump's remarks. Oil costs had soared since the war, putting heavy political pressure on Trump.
There was no immediate response from Iran or Israel. Iran had previously said it was ready for any eventuality and defiantly refused to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, which it closed in retaliation for the attack launched on Feb 28.
Trump had set a deadline of 8pm Washington time (midnight GMT), or 3.30am in Tehran, after an earlier extension in a threat to destroy all power plants and bridges across the country of 90 million people – a war crime against sites that are primarily of civilian usage.
Trump earlier Tuesday made threats shocking even by his own provocative standards that brought warnings that he was encouraging genocide – potentially one day leading to war crimes charges against US servicemembers who comply.
"A whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again. I don't want that to happen, but it probably will," Trump had written.
The rhetoric was an escalation from a profanity-laden post two days earlier, on Easter Sunday.
Pope Leo XIV said that "this threat against all the people of Iran" was "truly unacceptable."
- Pakistani mediation -
Pakistan, which is playing a key mediating role in the Middle East conflict, said it had proposed the two-week extension of Trump's deadline and that Iran should also reopen the Strait of Hormuz for the same period as a "goodwill gesture."
[[nid:1412648]]
"Diplomatic efforts for peaceful settlement of the ongoing war in the Middle East are progressing steadily, strongly and powerfully with the potential to lead to substantive results in near future," Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said.
The United States and Israel struck key infrastructure even before Trump's deadline, with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu confirming attacks on railways and bridges he said were "used by the Revolutionary Guards."
The Israeli military also offered a rare statement of regret after it acknowledged damaging a synagogue in Tehran, saying it had been targeting a senior Iranian commander.
Iran, run by Shia Muslim clerics, is home to around 100 synagogues for its historic Jewish minority.
Infrastructure attacks reported by Iranian authorities Tuesday included a US-Israeli strike on a bridge outside the city of Qom and another on a rail bridge in central Iran that killed two people.
- Death 'not a joke' -
University student Metanat, whose classmate was killed two weeks ago in an attack, told AFP before Trump's suspension of the bombing she felt "terrified and so should everyone else in the country."
The 27-year-old, who declined to give her last name, said as far as Trump's ultimatums were concerned, "some people think they are a joke", but "death is not a joke."
State media published photos purporting to show groups of Iranians forming human chains to protect power plants.
The show of patriotism in the face of attacks came several months after Iran's cleric-run government cracked down violently on mass protests, with rights groups reporting thousands of deaths.
The United States and Israel said that they attacked Iran to degrade its military capacity. Trump has alleged that Iran was near building an atomic bomb, an assertion not backed by the UN nuclear watchdog and most observers.
At the UN Security Council, Russia and China vetoed a resolution on reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a text already diluted to remove the green light Gulf states had sought to use force to protect the key shipping lane.
Apart from the infrastructure attacks, strikes were reported on Kharg island, a critical hub for the Iranian oil industry, according to Iran's Mehr news agency, although US media said the attacks were against military targets.
Iran has responded to the war by striking Gulf Arab states that host US troops. Israel in turn has launched a major offensive into Lebanon, vowing to control land from which Iranian-linked Hizbullah has fired rockets. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rising living costs leave Penang's public housing threshold outdated, warns expert
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The RM1,500 household income threshold for  People's Housing Programme (PPR) in the state has been questioned by experts for failing to reflect rising costs of living.
Associate Professor Dr Md Azree Othuman Mydin, dean of the School of Housing, Building and Planning at Universiti Sains Malaysia, said the benchmark "was reasonable in the past".
"But with inflation and rising housing costs, households earning just above RM1,500 can still face financial hardship," he said.
He highlighted  borderline families — households marginally above the threshold —   struggling with  basic needs and  urged a flexible approach  considersing medical costs, household size and other financial burdens.
"Transitional support, including tiered subsidies, financial advice and job aid, can help families achieve  financial stability."
Azree, who is also a registered building surveyor, also advocated for a graduated rent system to prevent immediate eviction.
He said gradually increasing rent  with household income, providing transitional units, relocation assistance and employment support could reduce displacement risks and prevent families from being forced into unsafe or unaffordable housing.
"Immediate eviction can destabilise families. Financial counselling and transitory housing options are essential to ensure a seamless transition for marginal tenants."
He also highlighted the importance of appeal mechanisms for tenants to dispute their ineligibility and  state guidance  to secure alternative affordable accommodation.
Safety and accessibility standards for the elderly and disabled must also be enforced, he added.
He said the three-year occupancy guideline had been inadequately enforced in the past.
"This has limited access for new applicants. The recent drive to enforce these rules is a step towards equitable housing access.".
Azree also proposed exit pathways, including rent-to-own schemes, low-interest loans, down payment assistance, financial literacy programmes and transitional housing.
He said partnerships with private developers could provide sustainable long-term housing options for these families.
Comparing Penang's PPR with other programmes, he said the RM1,500 threshold was far below the federal standard thresholds such as Sarawak's RM5,000.
He said programmes like Rumah MutiaraKu allowed incomes of up to RM12,000.
"Internationally, public housing eligibility often depends on a percentage of income rather than a fixed amount, with savings schemes like Singapore's Central Provident Fund enhancing accessibility."
Azree also said systemic barriers prevented tenants from moving to home ownership or the private rental market.
He said the barriers included  low wages, rising property prices, limited financial literacy, job insecurity, pre-existing debt, scarce affordable housing and stringent lending requirements.
He stressed the importance of long-term social housing for those unable to transition out permanently.
"Income assessments should include contributions from all household members, and gradual rent adjustments can support multi-generational households as children begin earning."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Screams of delight': Artemis crew flying home to thrilled NASA scientists
Article:
HOUSTON, (Texas): The Artemis II astronauts were jetting towards Earth and sharing their lunar flyby souvenirs Tuesday, delighting colleagues both at home and in space with captivating tales of their historic journey.
Of note was their stunning shot of an Earthset, the moment when Earth drops below the rugged lunar horizon.
The image was a deliberate nod to the iconic Earthrise image taken during the Apollo 8 mission in 1968, a shot that helped capture the public's imagination as humans traveled around the Moon for the first time.
The modern version released by NASA and the White House pairs the delicate, watery planet Earth with the harsh curve of the Moon, separated by black space.
The astronauts were busy Tuesday afternoon debriefing lunar scientists in Houston's mission control about what they had witnessed during their nearly seven-hour observation period of the Moon.
"Our whole lunar science team and the broader science community have been pouring out positive feedback and gratitude, so know that what you did yesterday really made a difference scientifically," Kelsey Young, the lunar science lead for the Artemis II mission, told the crew.
Speaking to a briefing later on, Young said "spirits are very high."
"We did what we set out to do."
The crew of four — US astronauts Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch, and Victor Glover along with Canadian Jeremy Hansen — looped around Earth's natural satellite as part of a broader program paving the way for a Moon landing as soon as 2028.
After completing their lunar flyby — in which they also broke the record for distance from Earth — they were bound for home, with splashdown due in the Pacific off the California coast late Friday.
In the meantime they took a call from colleagues in space — chatting with crewmembers aboard the International Space Station.
"It's fun to be up in space with you at the same time!" said Hansen.
Tonight they'll have another chance to speak with their families, NASA said.
- 'Screams of delight' -
The crew have reported in vivid detail features of the lunar surface and later witnessed a solar eclipse, when the Moon passed in front of the Sun.
They also described flashes of light — meteor strikes — on the Moon's surface.
"Humans probably have not evolved to see what we're seeing," said Victor Glover. "It is truly hard to describe. It is amazing."
Young reported that there were "audible screams of delight" in NASA's Science Evaluation Room as the astronauts evoked their observations.
Late Monday, the crew got a congratulatory call from NASA administrator Jared Isaacman and US President Donald Trump, who at 79 is old enough to remember the Apollo program.
He was, in a word, impressed.
"You've really inspired the entire world," Trump said, calling them "modern-day pioneers" who have "a lot of courage."
He probed the astronauts about their favorite moments and asked what it was like to lose connection with Earth for some 40 minutes during an expected communications blackout.
The call even had a little signal glitch of its own, with Trump waiting expectantly as the astronauts appeared not to hear him for a full minute.
"Might have gotten cut off," Trump said afterwards. "It is a long distance."
- Historic trip -
The Artemis II team broke the distance record set by the 1970 Apollo 13 mission, which they surpassed by more than 4,000 miles (more than 6,000 kilometers) when they reached the journey's furthest distance from Earth 252,756 miles (406,771 kilometers).
Glover was the first person of color to fly around the Moon, Koch was the first woman, and Canadian Hansen the first non-American.
The Orion capsule carrying the astronauts is now traveling back to Earth in a so-called free-return trajectory ahead of its ocean splashdown.
Administrator Isaacman said the ship that will recover the astronauts has left port en route to the meeting point. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HBA backs firm action on PPR subletting abuse
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The National House Buyers Association (HBA) has called for stricter enforcement against abuse of People's Housing Programme (PPR) units, while urging  a measured approach towards tenants who may no longer qualify.
Honorary secretary-general Datuk Chang Kim Loong said firm action was needed to ensure  subsidised housing reached  intended low-income groups.
"HBA supports the authorities' firm stand, which benefits  future tenants, especially given the long queue for social housing meant for eligible groups," he said.
On Monday, state Housing and Environment Committee chairman Datuk Seri S. Sundarajoo said more than 30 per cent of PPR tenants in the state would  be asked to vacate their units after being found no longer eligible, in a move to ensure  fairer access to low-cost housing.
He said the decision followed a comprehensive data "cleansing"  to ensure that only qualified households continued to benefit.
In an initial enforcement exercise, notices had been issued to 18 tenants of the Taman Manggis PPR who had overstayed beyond the stipulated period or whose household income exceeded the RM1,500  threshold.
Sundarajoo  said PPR units were not intended as permanent housing, but  as temporary support — typically limited to three years — to help families stabilise financially before transitioning to home ownership or other housing options.
Chang concurred with Sundarajoo, adding  that  misuse,  particularly  subletting,  had persisted for years.
"This deviates  from the  purpose of PPR housing. These units are meant to  shelter  the needy, not  for profit," he added, adding that enforcement had at times been inconsistent.
He said tenants found breaching tenancy terms, including subletting, should face termination.
At the same time, Chang said enforcement must be accompanied by fair and structured exit policies.
On households that fall just above eligibility thresholds, he said the state executive council  could consider  temporary extensions.
"At the same time, the interests of those on the waiting list must also be safeguarded."
He said HBA did not support  higher rental tiers for such tenants, citing the lack of clear limits and the risk of undermining the social housing framework.
To ensure continued eligibility, Chang proposed periodic "means testing", with reviews every three years to assess tenants' financial standing.
"Those  no longer eligible should give way to others who qualify, but this should not leave  families without housing."
He also called for a formal appeal mechanism to allow tenants to contest decisions, with cases  assessed individually based on supporting documents.
To address potential displacement, Chang suggested  authorities work with private developers to identify unsold or overhang units that could be offered as alternative rental options.
HBA also reiterated  support for rent-to-own (RTO) schemes as a pathway to homeownership, particularly for lower- and middle-income groups.
Chang said certain PPR units could be considered for conversion into RTO schemes, while overhang properties could  be used to expand such programmes.
He said effective RTO schemes should include clearly defined pricing, affordable rental terms and transparency on future purchase commitments.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cheras karaoke riot signals rising criminal boldness, says criminologist
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:     A violent daytime attack on an entertainment outlet in Taman Shamelin Perkasa, Cheras, involving more than 50 masked individuals, has raised alarm.
The incident saw attackers forcing their way into a
karaoke centre
using a lorry before causing extensive damage and fleeing in several cars,
Kuala Lumpur police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus said yesterday police acted swiftly to arrest 12 people.
However, an expert said the fact that the incident took place showed a troubling shift towards more brazen and organised forms of crime.
Senior criminologist Datuk Dr P. Sundramoorthy said the incident pointed to a level of coordination and intent that went beyond spontaneous violence.
"This is not an isolated act of vandalism. The scale, speed and coordination suggest clear premeditation and possibly organised group involvement," he told the
New Straits Times
.
He said the willingness of such a large group to carry out an attack in broad daylight reflected a growing sense of criminal boldness and a possible perception that enforcement risks were manageable.
"In criminological terms, this can be viewed as symbolic violence. It is not just about destruction but also about sending a message of intimidation and dominance, whether to rivals or other stakeholders."
Sundramoorthy said several indicators pointed towards syndicate or gang-related activity, including the use of disguises, the number of participants and the targeted nature of the premises.
"At the same time, we must be cautious about drawing premature conclusions. The location and target may suggest underlying disputes, whether commercial or territorial, but these need to be established through investigation."
He said the incident also raised questions about gaps in policing.
"The ability of such a large group to mobilise and execute an operation without early detection suggests limitations in surveillance and intelligence-led policing," he said.
"Modern law enforcement must increasingly focus on identifying patterns, networks and early warning signs."
Sundramoorthy highlighted the challenges of relying on informants in cases involving loosely organised or rapidly mobilised groups but stressed  human intelligence remained critical.
"This underscores the importance of strengthening informant networks, encouraging community reporting and enhancing inter-agency information sharing."
On response capabilities, he said the group's ability to carry out the attack and escape swiftly indicated a narrow window for intervention, pointing to the need for faster deployment and improved coordination among enforcement agencies.
"There is also a need to leverage real-time surveillance technologies more effectively to shorten response times," he added.
From a legal standpoint, Sundramoorthy said existing  laws on rioting and unlawful assembly provided a basis for prosecution, but incidents of this scale might prompt broader  discussions.
"Some jurisdictions have introduced specific anti-gang laws to address organised violence. Whether Malaysia needs similar frameworks is a question worth examining."
He said beyond physical damage, such incidents also carried significant psychological impact.
"When violence is carried out so openly, it can erode public confidence and create a perception of insecurity. Restoring trust requires not just arrests, but also visible enforcement and clear communication from the authorities."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Klang security guard dies after assailant returns a second time to attack
Article:
KLANG: The attack that killed a security supervisor at a residential area in Bandar Bukit Raja here was a senseless act of violence, said his colleague, describing how an  initial argument later turned fatal.
The victim, G. Anand, a 43-year-old father of two boys, was rushed to Shah Alam Hospital about 1am on Sunday but succumbed to his injuries.
His supervisor, D. Viswa, 35, said the assailant came back to the guard post not once, but twice.
"The first time, his friends who were with him calmed him down. He, however, returned alone minutes later and attacked Anand with a steering lock," said Viswa when met at Anand's funeral at  Fairy Park Heritage City yesterday.
He said the blow to Anand's rib area proved fatal.
Recounting the moments leading up to the incident, Viswa said Anand had been carrying out his routine patrols when he noticed three men drinking in the compound of a rented house in the neighbourhood.
"As part of his duty, he approached the area and stopped to observe what was taking place.
"One of the men, who appeared intoxicated, shouted at him, asking, 'What are you staring at?'," he said.
Another man quickly intervened and apologised, telling Anand to leave as his friend was drunk. Anand then returned to his guard post.
However, the situation escalated when the suspect returned in a car with his friends.
The man's companions again prevented any confrontation but the man later came back alone and attacked Anand.
Police said the man and the victim did not know each other and that the case was not linked to gang activity, describing it as a random act believed to have been triggered by a man who was intoxicated.
Anand, who worked two jobs to support his family, was described by loved ones as a devoted husband and father to two boys, aged 9 and 13.
"By day, he was employed at a production company.
"By night, he took on supervisory duties as a security guard to make ends meet," said his elder brother, Segar.
Segar, 50, said his youngest brother was very close to him and had been working extra hard to provide for his sons.
"He just took on this job because of financial issues."
Anand's 44-year-old sister, Malar, said the family was struggling to come to terms with the sudden loss.
"He was a loving father and a caring brother. Everything he did was for his family," she said.
The suspect is believed to be a tenant at the new residential neighbourhood.
He has since been detained by police to facilitate investigations and is remanded until tomorrow.
Klang Utara police chief Assistant Commissioner S. Vijaya Rao earlier said the 40-year-old suspect had a criminal record with 10 cases, including for drugs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel army 'regrets' damage to Teheran synagogue, says targeted senior commander
Article:
JERUSALEM: The Israeli military on Tuesday expressed regret over "collateral damage" to a synagogue in Tehran caused by an overnight strike that it said was targeting a senior Iranian commander.
"Last night (Tuesday), the IDF struck a senior commander in the emergency headquarters 'Khatam al-Anbiya' of the Iranian regime," an Israeli military spokesperson said in response to a question from AFP.
"The IDF regrets the collateral damage to the synagogue and emphasises that the strike was targeted at a senior military target within the regime's armed forces, not at any place of worship."
The military said the results of the strike were "under review", adding that "steps were taken to mitigate harm to civilians, including the use of precise munitions and aerial surveillance."
Earlier on Tuesday, Iran's Shargh newspaper and Mehr news agency reported that the Rafi-Nia Synagogue in central Teheran had been "completely destroyed."
Judaism is one of Iran's legally recognised minority religions, and the country has a small Jewish community, although many members fled in the aftermath of the 1979 Islamic revolution.
There are no publicly available official numbers, but there are thought to be a few thousand Jewish people in Iran. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Petronas tanker picking up speed after clearing Hormuz Strait
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A Petronas tanker carrying Iraqi crude oil has picked up speed in the Arabian Sea, about 200 nautical miles off Oman, and is expected to arrive in Pengerang on April 17, Marine Traffic showed.
The
Ocean Thunder
, chartered by Petco, a unit of Petronas, is among seven Malaysia-linked vessels cleared by Iran to transit the Strait of Hormuz.
The tanker left Al Basrah Port in southern Iraq on April 3.
Iraq's main deepwater port handles most of the country's imports and exports, including oil and industrial goods.
[[nid:1412644]]
A veteran captain familiar with sailing in the Persian Gulf told the
New Straits Times
that the
Ocean Thunder
's successful transit of the strait reflected strong ties between Malaysia and Iran.
Since United States-‑Israeli air strikes on Iran began on Feb  28, the vital Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed to most shipping. The narrow strait carries about a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas flows.
In recent days, three Omani-operated tankers, a French-owned container ship and a Japanese-owned gas carrier have also crossed the strait.
The
Ocean Thunder
, a crude oil tanker sailing under the Panamanian flag, is 274.2m long and 50.04m wide.
With its speed rising to 13.3 knots from 12.8 knots, it will sail across the Arabian Sea, through the Bay of Bengal and into the Strait of Malacca.
Marine conditions off Oman are calm to moderate, with light easterly to southeasterly winds.
[[nid:1412643]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tahfiz schools lift Pahang to 2nd place in 2025 SPM
Article:
KUANTAN:  Outstanding performances in the 2025 Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) by Maahad Tahfiz Negeri Pahang (MTNP) and Maahad Tahfiz Turath Negeri Pahang (MTTNP), both under Yayasan Pahang, have helped the state achieve its best-ever state average grade, placing it second nationwide.
MTTNP emerged as the top school in both Pahang and the country, while MTNP was ranked second in the state and placed among the top five nationwide.
MTTNP recorded an impressive school average grade (GPS) of 0.6, with 83.3 per cent of its students achieving straight A's, underscoring the institution's status as a model
of educational excellence that integrates academic strength with character development.
MTNP, the top-performing school nationwide in the 2023 SPM, delivered another outstanding performance, recording a GPS of 1.06 — the best in its history — with 90 per cent of its students scoring at least eight A's.
Yayasan Pahang said some quarters had argued that religious subjects such as Al-Adab Wa Al-Balaghah and Hifz Al-Quran contributed to inflated grades.
"This is untrue. Unlike typical science stream students who usually take about nine subjects, students at MTNP often handle  11 to 12 subjects.
"This includes advanced Arabic and specialised Islamic studies, indicating that their achievements are not derived from taking 'easier' papers, but from managing a more demanding curriculum."
The foundation said greater emphasis should be placed on the cognitive impact of
hifz
, or Quran memorisation, as studies suggested that the discipline required to memorise all 30
juzuk
(chapters) could  boost focus and memory retention.
"These cognitive benefits support students in mastering challenging subjects such as Physics and Additional Mathematics, as well as passing the Syahadah Quran tests for 10, 20 and 30
juzuk
," it said.
Yayasan Pahang said beyond academic performance, students were shaped by a rigorous culture of discipline and time management.
"Their daily routine, often described as a '3am to 11pm cycle', reflects a balance between religious and academic commitments.
"The day typically begins in the early hours before dawn with Quran memorisation, requiring high levels of focus and mental endurance.
"As the day progresses, students transition into formal academic classes, including practical science laboratory work.
"This demonstrates their ability to manage time effectively in a dual curriculum that integrates spiritual and academic excellence.
"The strong SPM performance was attributed to the commitment of stakeholders in advancing education in the state, particularly under the leadership of Yayasan Pahang chairman Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Wan Rosdy Wan Ismail.
The achievement reflects steady progress in the state's education sector, demonstrating that ongoing efforts to improve quality are yielding results.
This aligns with the Pahang First aspiration, which prioritises human capital development and educational excellence.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: French couple leave Iran after more than three years in detention
Article:
PARIS: Two French nationals were headed home from war-stricken Iran on Tuesday after spending more than three years in an Iranian prison on espionage charges, French President Emmanuel Macron said.
Cecile Kohler, 41, and Jacques Paris, 72, had been under house arrest at the French embassy in Teheran ever since they were freed in November, with their fate even more uncertain after US-Israeli strikes on Iran started on February 28.
"Cecile Kohler and Jacques Paris are free and on their way to France, after three and a half years in detention in Iran," the French president wrote on X.
A French foreign ministry source, speaking on condition of anonymity, said the couple left Iran at dawn Tuesday in a diplomatic convoy with the French ambassador and "are currently in Azerbaijan."
The pair are set to return to France on Wednesday after spending the evening in the capital of Baku, according to the same source, though no further details were provided.
They were the last two French nationals being held in Iran.
In a separate case, Iranian national Mahdieh Esfandiari remains in France, her lawyer Nabil Boudi told AFP on Tuesday, despite previous suggestions from Tehran that she could be exchanged for Kohler and Paris in a possible prisoner swap.
Esfandiari was sentenced by a French court in February to one year in prison for justifying terrorism over comments she made on social media, including on Palestinian group Hamas's attack on Israel on Oct 7, 2023.
But her house arrest order was lifted following the French couple's departure from Iran, her lawyer told AFP.
A source from Macron's office said on Tuesday that Esfandiari was a "separate case", noting she had lodged an appeal, without offering further details.
The French government has never confirmed the existence of a potential exchange deal.
- 'Tears in my eyes' -
Kohler and Paris – both teachers, although Paris is retired – were arrested in May 2022 at the end of a trip to Iran that their families say was purely touristic in nature.
At the end of a closed-door trial, an Iranian court in October sentenced them to jail on espionage charges their families say were fabricated.
It jailed Paris for 17 years and Kohler 20 years for allegedly spying for France and Israel.
They were released the following month.
Officials and their supporters celebrated the news of their return to France.
"We are waiting for their return to France so we can give them a big hug," Anne-Laure Paris, Jacques Paris's daughter, told AFP.
French Foreign Minister Jean-Noel Barrot said the couple were "free at last" and that he spoke with them on the phone.
"They told me of their emotion and joy at soon being reunited with their country and their loved ones," Barrot wrote on X.
The lawyer for their support committee, Thierry Moser, told AFP that he was "overjoyed." "I have tears in my eyes, I'm almost struggling to speak," he said.
The pair were among a number of Europeans caught up in what activists and some Western governments describe as a deliberate strategy of hostage-taking by Iran to extract concessions from the West.
They left after US President Donald Trump on Monday warned of widespread strikes on civilian infrastructure after the expiry of a deadline he issued for the Islamic republic to reopen the vital Strait of Hormuz. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: To Lam elected president
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
VIETNAM'S lawmakers yesterday unanimously elected Communist Party secretary-general To Lam as the country's state president for the next five years, making him the most powerful Vietnamese leader in decades.
The widely anticipated move marks a break from Vietnam's traditional collective leadership system, consolidating authority in one figure in ways analysts say could tilt the one-party state towards greater authoritarianism, while also enabling faster decision-making, similar to its neighbour China.
The Parliament endorsed the Communist Party's nomination, which officials said was finalised in a meeting at the end of last month.
The former head of public security now has a double mandate to rule the country for the next five years, after he secured a second term as secretary-general in January.
After the vote, Lam said it was an honour to hold both posts and pledged "a new growth model with science, technology, innovation and digital transformation as the primary driving forces".
He also said he would prioritise self-reliance in defence.
"Concentrating greater power in To Lam's hands could pose risks to Vietnam's political system, such as increased authoritarianism," said Le Hong Hiep, senior fellow at the ISEAS Yusof Ishak Institute in Singapore.
However, such consolidation "could enable Vietnam to formulate and implement policies more quickly and effectively", supporting growth, he added.
The combination of the two roles "will shift Vietnam's domestic politics to a new normal where most of the old assumptions about Vietnam's politics, including those about collective leadership, are no longer valid", said Alexander Vuving of the Asia-Pacific Centre for Security Studies in the United States.
Lam held both posts for a few months following the death of the late party secretary-general Nguyen Phu Trong in 2024.
Even after relinquishing the state presidency in favour of army general Luong Cuong, Lam often acted as if he had retained the role, representing the country in meetings with foreign leaders.
In his first stint as party chief, Lam, 68, launched sweeping economic reforms designed to make Vietnam more competitive, which drew both praise and criticism.
He has vowed to pursue double-digit growth through a new development model that is less reliant on low-cost manufacturing, long the backbone of Vietnam's export-driven boom and led by foreigners.
Lam's moves have at times unsettled the administration and businesses, but he has shown a pragmatic flexibility in executing them.
He has backed the expansion of private conglomerates, but before his reappointment, also issued a directive emphasising the leading role of state-owned enterprises to reassure party traditionalists.
Foreign investors, a key component of Vietnam's export-reliant economy, have often praised the country's political stability and see Lam as a pro-business leader.
However, his backing of national champions and push for breakneck growth have raised concerns among some about favouritism, corruption risks, asset bubbles and waste.
In foreign policy, Lam has also been pragmatic.
He has maintained Vietnam's "Bamboo Diplomacy" and sought to balance relations with major powers while expanding international partnerships.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI disruption dims outlook for smartphone market
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: The global smartphone market is set to face a sharper downturn as artificial intelligence (AI) reshapes demand and pressures manufacturers, according to MBSB Investment Bank Bhd (MBSB Research).
The weakening smartphone demand alongside broader supply chain and geopolitical risks prompted MBSB Research to maintain its "neutral" view on the technology sector.
MBSB Research said the outlook for smartphones is deteriorating as AI and high-performance computing advance. This pushes up input costs and forcing manufacturers to adjust pricing strategies and device specifications.
Industry trackers International Data Corporation and Counterpoint Research  project global smartphone shipments to fall by more than 10 per cent year-on-year in 2026 —  the weakest level in a decade.
"Global smartphone demand continues to soften as AI-driven device upgrades and pricing pressures reshape the market dynamics," MBSB Research said.
At the same time, supply chain risks are rising beyond existing helium constraints, with Middle East tensions posing potential disruptions to sulphur supply, a key input for sulphuric acid used in semiconductor wafer production.
While the broader semiconductor industry is still expected to grow this year, MBSB Research cautioned that geopolitical risks could weigh on overall electronics sales.
It also flagged rising uncertainty in the Middle East, which includes reported threats to United States technology firms in the region.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perhilitan tracking tiger after paw prints found at farm
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KLUANG: The Wildlife and National Parks Department (Perhilitan) has stepped up patrols following the discovery of tiger paw prints at a farm here last Friday.
Perhilitan director-general Datuk Abdul Kadir Abu Hashim said a four-member team from the Batu Pahat and Kluang Perhilitan offices confirmed that the prints belonged to a Malayan tiger.
The tracks, estimated to be three to four days old, measured about 12cm wide and 11cm long, consistent with a sub-adult or adult tiger.
The farm where the tracks were found is located about 15km from the Renggam Forest Reserve and 27km from the Kluang Forest Reserve.
"We will step up patrols in the area for the next 14 days," said Perhilitan.
Its Malayan Tiger Conservation Unit will also deploy camera traps to track the animal's movements and determine the appropriate follow-up action.
Residents and workers have been urged to remain vigilant, avoid venturing alone into plantations or forest fringes and limit outdoor activity during the early morning and late evening.
Kadir also called on the public to report sightings or share any information via its hotline or official complaint channels.
The Malayan tiger has been sighted near human settlements more regularly in recent years, often due to habitat encroachment and dwindling prey.
A survey between 2016 and 2020 by Perhilitan and conservation groups estimates there are fewer than 150 Malayan tigers in the wild.
In 2021, the government formed the National Tiger Conservation Task Force to arrest the decline of the animal, which features on Malaysia's coat of arms.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt revives plan for independent National Harmony Commission
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
THE government is reviving a plan to establish an independent National Harmony Commission, with the National Unity Ministry backing a renewed proposal amid growing concerns over unity-related issues.
The ministry said the move followed a proposal by activist Tan Sri Lee Lam Thye and builds on earlier efforts dating back to 2015, when the National Unity Advisory Council drafted three bills on racial and religious hatred offences, national harmony and reconciliation and the establishment of a commission.
However, in 2019, the government rejected two of the bills due to overlaps with existing laws and constitutional provisions, including Article 8 of the Federal Constitution, on non-discrimination.
While the commission proposal was agreed to in principle, it later stalled over jurisdictional concerns involving agencies such as the Human Rights Commission of Malaysia and the police.
The government subsequently decided in April 2020 not to proceed.
The ministry said efforts were now being revived through the National Unity and Integration Department, with stakeholder engagement sessions already under way since early this month.
"The proposed commission will prioritise prevention and reconciliation through early intervention, mediation and constructive dispute resolution, rather than punitive measures alone," said the ministry.
If established, the commission will be empowered to investigate matters affecting harmony, summon relevant parties, issue recommendations, facilitate mediation and publish reports, subject to national security considerations.
It is also expected to draw on international models, including Singapore's Maintenance of Religious Harmony Act, as well as frameworks used by the Australian Human Rights Commission and Zimbabwe's National Peace and Reconciliation Commission.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK govt bars Kanye West, forcing cancellation of festival
Article:
LONDON: The UK government on Tuesday blocked US rapper Kanye West from entering the country due to his past antisemitic outbursts, prompting organisers of a festival he was to headline to cancel the July event.
The disgraced rapper had submitted Monday an application to travel to Britain, but it was refused on the grounds that his presence would not be conducive to the public good, a UK government source confirmed.
The organisers of the Wireless Festival in London where West – now known as Ye – had been booked to play all three nights in July swiftly cancelled the event.
"As a result of the Home Office banning YE from entering the United Kingdom, Wireless Festival has been forced to cancel," organisers said on Instagram.
They added all ticket holders would receive "an automatic full refund." Tickets had only gone on sale on Tuesday.
Controversy around West headlining the festival grew over the weekend, with a string of the event's sponsors pulling out, including drinks giants Pepsi and Diageo.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer, who on Saturday had called booking West "deeply concerning", said Tuesday that the 48-year-old hip-hop star "should never have been invited to headline Wireless."
- 'Poison' -
"This government stands firmly with the Jewish community, and we will not stop in our fight to confront and defeat the poison of antisemitism," Starmer added on X.
"We will always take the action necessary to protect the public and uphold our values."
West, who last performed in Britain when he headlined the Glastonbury festival in 2015, has been heavily criticised for making antisemitic remarks and voicing admiration for Adolf Hitler.
In May 2025, he released a song called "Heil Hitler", months after advertising a swastika t-shirt for sale on his website.
The song was banned by major streaming platforms.
The US rapper subsequently expressed regret for his conduct, blaming it on his bipolar disorder.
As the row around his planned London performance heated, West had offered to meet members of Britain's Jewish community.
In an advert in the Wall Street Journal headlined "To Those I've Hurt", West said his "only goal" was "to present a show of change, bringing unity, peace, and love through my music."
- 'Lessons' -
"I would be grateful for the opportunity to meet with members of the Jewish community in the UK in person," the rapper added.
Phil Rosenberg, president of the Board of Deputies of British Jews, welcomed the government intervention but said "the situation could and should have been resolved much earlier."
"We hope that lessons are learned across the industry," he added.
"Music festivals should be places where all communities feel welcome, not venues that platform individuals with records of profiteering from antisemitism, racism and other repulsive views."
But Nigel Farage, leader of the hard-right Reform UK party and a critic of so-called cancel culture, branded such bans "a dangerous path to go down."
"If we start banning people from entering the country because we don't like what they say, I worry where that ends up," he said, while calling West's past comments "really vile."
Farage has in recent months been dogged by antisemitism allegations dating back to his school days.
West's European comeback tour had already provoked controversy prior to the furore in Britain.
In France, the mayor of Marseille said the rapper was "not welcome" for a concert planned there in June. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran cuts direct diplomacy with US as Trump's deadline looms
Article:
ANKARA: Iran has cut off direct communications with the US following President Donald Trump's threat to destroy Iran's "whole civilisation", Anadolu Ajansi reported, citing media reports on Tuesday.
The move came ahead of a Tuesday deadline set by Trump for a potential agreement, complicating efforts to reach a deal but not ending ongoing negotiations, according to The New York Times, citing three Iranian officials.
Officials said indirect talks through ceasefire mediators are continuing despite the suspension of direct contact, according to the reporting, which was largely confirmed by The Wall Street Journal.
One official said Iran's decision was intended to send "a signal of disapproval and defiance" in response to the threat, according to the outlet.
It remains unclear whether direct communication between Tehran and Washington will resume before the deadline.
The White House did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Trump earlier warned that "a whole civilisation will die tonight, never to be brought back again" — remarks that fuelled speculation as his 8pm Eastern Time (0000 GMT Wednesday) deadline for Iran approaches. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Only the president knows' what he'll do on Iran -White House
Article:
WASHINGTON: Donald Trump is the only person who knows his plans for Iran, the White House said, after the US president warned that a "whole civilisation will die' if Tehran fails to reach a deal Tuesday.
"The Iranian regime has until 8pm Eastern Time to meet the moment and make a deal with the United States," Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said in a statement to AFP, when asked if Trump was prepared to use a nuclear weapon, and about a report that Iran had cut off negotiations.
"Only the President knows where things stand and what he will do," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rantau Panjang villagers buy water tank as dry taps persist
Article:
RANTAU PANJANG: Villagers in Kampung Che Daah, Gual Tok Deh, have pooled their money to buy a 400-litre water tank costing RM430, as months of dry taps continue to disrupt daily life.
The community has been relying on the state government's water operator, Air Kelantan Sdn Bhd (AKSB), to fill the tank whenever supply trucks arrive.
However, deliveries are too infrequent to meet the needs of 200 to 300 families in the area.
Villager Muhamed Dollah, 64, said the water shortage began months before Ramadan and remains unresolved.
"Our well has dried up, and even the tube well is no longer producing water. AKSB comes only once every two or three days.
"Therefore, we were forced to find alternatives. We decided to pool our money to buy the tank and ask them to fill it, but it is still not enough because there are so many of us."
Residents are now urging AKSB to send water daily to ease their burden.
"How are our schoolchildren supposed to bathe early in the morning if the situation persists? We have no choice but to use whatever water we have, even from the Golok River, for our daily needs," Dollah added.
For 80-year-old Fatimah Ahmad, life has been particularly challenging.
"We have had no water supply since January. Now, we depend on the tank, but it is never enough.
"Every day, we have to queue to collect water. Sometimes, we only take a small bottle just to perform our prayers," she said.
Che Daud Che Mat, 68, said the village's small and remote nature may have caused it to be overlooked.
"Our village is small, and sometimes people overlook us.
"We hope the authorities will show sympathy to residents here. For now, we have to depend on the Golok River, but the relevant agencies must take responsibility," he said.
Che Daud called on the authorities, including the Health Ministry, to implement long-term solutions, such as installing dedicated tube wells and larger storage tanks to serve the village's roughly 500 residents.
"We need a proper system so that this problem does not continue," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Netanyahu says Israel struck railways, bridges in Iran
Article:
JERUSALEM: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Israel struck on Tuesday railways and bridges in Iran "used by the Revolutionary Guards", after Iranian officials reported damage to at least two bridges and railway infrastructure.
"We are crushing the terror regime in Iran... with even greater vigour and with increasing force," Netanyahu said in a video released by his office.
"Yesterday, our pilots destroyed transport aircraft and dozens of helicopters at an Iranian Air Force base. Today they struck the railways and bridges used by the Revolutionary Guards."
In a statement, the Israeli military said it struck "eight bridge segments that were utilised by the Iranian terror regime's Armed Forces for transporting weapons and military equipment in several areas across Iran, including Tehran, Karaj, Tabriz, Kashan, and Qom."
Israeli military spokesman Brigadier General Effie Defrin said "air force pilots continue to deepen the damage to the Iranian terror regime."
"We eliminated several regime commanders last night and struck key infrastructure it used," he wrote in a post on X.
The announcement of the strikes came as US President Donald Trump warned "a whole civilisation will die" if a midnight deadline for a deal to open the Strait of Hormuz was not met.
Trump had warned that unless Tehran allowed free passage through the strategic oil chokepoint by midnight GMT, the United States would unleash what he called the "complete demolition" of Iran's critical infrastructure, including bridges and power plants. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST receives top English-language coverage award from UPM
Article:
SERDANG: The New Straits Times has received the Highest English-Language Coverage Award at the Universiti Putra Malaysia (UPM) Media Engagement and Promotion Ceremony 2026 here tonight.
The awards were presented in recognition of the newspaper's extensive coverage of UPM, including news reports, expert contributions and knowledge-sharing articles in the print media.
Receiving the awards on behalf of NST was Associate Editor (Content and Bureaus, Digital and Print) Izwan Ismail.
Besides NST, other media organisations also received awards at the ceremony.
Berita Harian received the Highest Bahasa Melayu-Language Coverage Award and the Print Media Visibility Award, while Harian Metro received the Highest Bahasa Melayu-Language Coverage Award and the Highest Research News Coverage Award.
TV3 won the Highest TV Coverage Award and the Special Recognition Award for Talk Show Programme.
Bernama was recognised for the highest overall media coverage of UPM, taking home a plaque, certificate of appreciation and RM1,500 in cash.
Presenting the awards was UPM vice-chancellor Datuk Prof Dr Ahmad Farhan Mohd Sadullah, who said the role of the media in amplifying outreach, highlighting the university's expertise and communicating its achievements to the public is vital and deeply appreciated.
In his speech, Ahmad Farhan thanked media organisations for their continued support, describing it as a key factor in strengthening the university's credibility and standing at both national and international levels.
"The impact would not be felt by the public without the cooperation of the media.
"Thank you for highlighting expertise and ideas that benefit society, particularly in the context of nation-building.
"Through this collaboration, UPM's expertise can also contribute to the country, especially in addressing key issues that require new ideas, approaches and solutions.
"UPM does not aspire to be merely an ivory tower, but a beacon of knowledge," he said at the event.
Ahmad Farhan said that in navigating current challenges, UPM's experts play a crucial role as a bridge in disseminating knowledge to society.
"Expertise should not be confined to academic publications alone, but must be translated into solutions that deliver tangible benefits to the nation and its people.
"This is where the role of media partners becomes especially significant, and UPM welcomes continued collaboration as strategic partners in conveying research findings, expertise and the university's achievements to the wider public.
"The media plays an important role in bridging the gap between academia and society.
"UPM hopes to produce more experts who can share their views, inspire others and elevate the university to greater heights," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Power cut off on Iran's Kharg Island after heavy US strikes: Reports
Article:
ISTANBUL: Power was cut off on Iran's Kharg Island in the Persian Gulf on Tuesday following a wave of US strikes targeting more than 50 sites, Anadolu Ajansi reported, citing US and Iranian media.
Iranian outlets said the power outage came after heavy US attacks on the island.
The Wall Street Journal said that US fighter jets bombed more than 50 military targets on the Iranian island.
Iran's Mehr News Agency earlier said that US and Israeli warplanes carried out several airstrikes on the island, causing multiple explosions.
No details were yet available about casualties or damage.
Last week, US President Donald Trump said that all military targets on Kharg Island "have been completely destroyed."
Trump also expressed interest in "controlling" Iranian oil, suggesting that seizing Kharg Island, Iran's main oil export terminal, was among the options under consideration.
Regional escalations have continued to rage since Israel and the US launched an offensive against Iran on Feb 28, killing more than 1,340 people, including then-Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. Iranian authorities have not updated the toll in recent days.
Iran has retaliated with drone and missile strikes targeting Israel, Jordan, Iraq, and Gulf countries hosting US military assets, causing casualties and damage to infrastructure while disrupting global markets and aviation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UPM strengthens food security through technology, youth involvement
Article:
SERDANG: Universiti Putra Malaysia (UPM) is helping to strengthen the national food security agenda through the use of modern technology and the involvement of the younger generation to enhance agricultural productivity and the quality of produce.
UPM vice-chancellor Datuk Prof Dr Ahmad Farhan Mohd Sadullah said the country's reliance on imported food and global geopolitical uncertainties underscored the importance of having domestic capability in food production to ensure a stable supply.
He said the use of technology in the agricultural sector was among the university's key strategies to increase output, accelerate production processes and reduce operating costs.
"We know that the profile of farmers in Malaysia largely consists of the older generation.
"Therefore, one of our strategies is to leverage technology through the younger workforce, with the hope that UPM graduates can transform the profile of farmers in Malaysia.
"Without technology, we will not be able to reduce costs, nor will we be able to improve yields and quality. So that is among the strategies we are implementing," he told reporters after UPM's event with the media here tonight.
Present was Malaysian National News Agency (Bernama) Chief Executive Officer Datin Paduka Nur-ul Afida Kamaludin.
Ahmad Farhan said UPM consistently worked closely with the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security to address issues and intensify local food production to reduce reliance on imports.
He said the university also provided training opportunities for farmers to enhance their skills and capacity through the sharing of expertise and the application of new technologies in the agricultural sector.
He said the effort was part of UPM's strategic agenda to help the country face economic challenges and ensure food security for the well-being of the people.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Timeline: Key events since Trump's first Hormuz deadline to Iran
Article:
ANKARA: A series of military escalations unfolded across West Asia after US President Donald Trump issued an initial deadline to Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, followed by repeated extensions and retaliatory strikes targeting key infrastructure and military assets, Anadolu Ajansi reported.
On March 21, Trump gave Iran 48 hours to reopen the strait, warning that US forces would strike the country's energy facilities if it failed to comply.
Two days later, on March 23, Trump said "very good and productive talks" had taken place and postponed the planned strike by five days.
On March 27, he extended the deadline further to April 6.
Hostilities escalated on March 29, when a US E-3 Sentry early warning aircraft was damaged on the ground during an airstrike at Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia.
On April 2, US and Israeli forces targeted the B1 Bridge linking Tehran and Karaj.
Between April 3 and 4, US F-15E and A-10 Warthog aircraft were downed, while two C-130 aircraft were destroyed during a rescue operation for a downed pilot.
Attacks on energy infrastructure continued on April 5. A strike on the Borouge petrochemical facility in Abu Dhabi caused a fire, while a UAV attack on an oil facility in Shuwaikh, Kuwait, also triggered a blaze.
The same day, Trump extended the deadline for an agreement with Iran to Tuesday, 8pm EDT (0000 GMT Wednesday).
On April 6, Iran's petrochemical facilities in Asaluyeh and the Shiraz Petrochemical Complex were targeted. A UAV attack on Ali Al-Salim Air Base injured 15 US soldiers.
The following day, April 7, a railway bridge in Isfahan was struck, while Kharg Island in the Persian Gulf also came under attack.
The developments highlight a rapid escalation in tensions across the region, with strikes affecting Iran, Israel, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait and the United Arab Emirates.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MTEN meeting reviews impact of global energy crisis, focus on protecting the people - Anwar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The National Economic Action Council (MTEN) meeting held today reviewed the impact of the global energy crisis, with the main focus on protecting the people and ensuring the security of the country's strategic supplies.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said efforts to curb leakages and smuggling would continue to be strengthened through tighter supply chain monitoring and the integrated implementation of Op Tiris 4.0 involving various enforcement agencies.
"The Madani Government also prioritises the security of medicine and medical device supplies through centralised stock monitoring, diversification of import sources and emergency plans to ensure critical needs are always sufficient," he said in a Facebook post.
Anwar, who is also the finance minister, said that in the long term, the government would strengthen strategic buffer stocks, develop local industries and formulate a comprehensive policy through MyMedSecure.
In addition, he said MTEN reviewed haj operations involving 31,600 Malaysian pilgrims, with priority given to their safety and welfare.
Anwar said the Madani Government would continue to act in an orderly and responsible manner to manage the crisis effectively while ensuring the people were protected and the country remained resilient.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: China's electronics manufacturer learns to live with Trump tariffs and thrive
Article:
President Donald Trump's tariffs sought to hurt Chinese manufacturing, but for one electronics maker, a turbulent 2025 ended with a belief that China is a location that is difficult to replicate — as long as things don't change too drastically.
Agilian Technology, which makes products mostly for Western brands, saw its US orders — accounting for more than half its revenue — frozen for months and clients demanded it set up production outside China.
Tariffs brought chaos to many Chinese companies but Beijing's retaliation — export controls on minerals and metals that US firms need and are difficult to source — reduced the levies.
This allowed Agilian, a US$30-million-a-year business, to recover  though it has pursued offshoring.
A recovery in China's manufacturing sector might surprise Trump following the anniversary of his "Liberation Day" tariff rollout, given that he campaigned on using levies to reindustrialise the American economy and project US power.
"The data confirms that Trump's tariffs indeed haven't derailed the momentum that we've seen in China's manufacturing sector," said Nick Marro, principal economist for Asia and lead for global trade at the Economist Intelligence Unit.
China's trade surplus for the first two months of 2026 rose to US$213.6 billion, official data showed, from US$169.21 billion a year earlier.
Gaussorgues wondered whether Trump would make a breakthrough when he visits China next month. Economists and industry executives expect Trump's visit to extend a detente between the two rivals.
According to Denis Depoux, the general manager of consultancy Roland Berger,  He Yadong, a spokesperson for China's Ministry of Commerce, said: "China has shown the rare earths (are) a leverage of mass destruction.
"It's a nuclear weapon of trade."
Today, Agilian executives view Trump's tariff policies as guideposts for how to deal with future flare-ups.
In 2024, as Trump was rising in election polls, Agilian's clients wanted to get ahead of tariffs and asked the firm to ship products to North American warehouses.
Other US importers had similar ideas and storage prices went "crazy", said Renaud Anjoran, the firm's vice-president.
Shortly after Trump was re-elected, post-midnight calls from "panicked" clients became frequent.  One customer with family in Penang urged Agilian to set up a production base there.
Agilian had set up an entity in India, but most clients pushed back on operating there, worried about slow production and Customs delays.
After Trump was inaugurated, two tariff hikes on China totalling 20 per cent concerned clients, but they stuck around. Then on April 2, tariffs on Chinese exports rose another 34 percentage points. For Agilian customers, "this was a disaster" and many cancelled orders.
China retaliated. Escalations pushed the levies above 100 per cent on both sides before the end of the month. "Things were frozen," said Anjoran.
The company decided to go with Penang and found a factory to partner with. It was preferred because it was removed from the South China Sea, where military conflicts can't be ruled out.
Agilian also scouted industrial rental space in Dharwad, India, and even looked at moving production to the US.
By mid-2025, Agilian's India team found a 4,000-square-metre industrial building and was discussing which products could be made there.
But then a May Washington-Beijing deal removed most of the tariffs imposed on China.
In August, Trump hiked tariffs on India by 50 per cent to force it to stop buying Russian oil.
But Anjoran pressed ahead: "We want to be a multi-country manufacturer. Focus on the long arc of time." Pre-production runs in Penang also started in the middle of the year.
Through the summer, China's export controls exposed US dependence on materials processed almost exclusively in China, squeezing autos, defence and other industries.
An October meeting between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping brought tariffs down by 10 percentage points. By then, Agilian's clients had stopped asking about levies and offshoring.
Agilian will keep developing facilities in India and Malaysia "as an insurance policy," Gaussorgues said.
He hopes to grow  revenue 30 per cent in the next three years, though he fears Trump could get in the way again.
"I started in January saying, okay, this might be a good year and then the Iran war started," he said.
The writer is from Reuters
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hormuz closure a boon for some Gulf states
Article:
THE Strait of Hormuz's closure and the resulting surge in global oil prices have handed financial windfalls to Iran, Oman and Saudi Arabia, while other states that lack alternative shipment routes have lost billions of dollars, a Reuters analysis found.
Iran effectively shut the strait — a route for about a fifth of global oil and LNG flows — after United States and Israeli air strikes on Iran at the end of February led to a widening conflict.
Energy markets experienced unprecedented disruption. While much of the world faces a surge in inflation and economic damage from the energy price rise, for the Middle Eastern oil producers, the impact has depended on their geography.
Although Iran has control over the strait, Oman, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates can bypass it via pipelines and ports, while oil from Iraq, Kuwait and Qatar have been trapped as they lack alternative routes to international markets.
Some analysts say the US-Israeli war on Iran in some ways has strengthened Teheran.
"Now that Hormuz has been closed, it can be closed again and again, and that poses a major threat to the global economy," said Neil Quilliam, associate fellow at think tank Chatham House.
Reuters' analysis of March export data found Iraq and Kuwait's estimated notional oil export revenues both plunged by about three-quarters year-on-year.
Conversely, Iran's revenues rose by 37 per cent and Oman's by 26 per cent. Saudi Arabia's oil revenues increased by 4.3 per cent, while the UAE's dipped by 2.6 per cent as the price surge offset lower volumes.
For Saudi Arabia, higher prices mean increased royalties and taxes from state oil giant Aramco.
The uplift is particularly positive for the kingdom following heavy spending on projects designed to diversify its income away from oil.
The kingdom's biggest pipeline is the 1,200km East-West link, built in the 1980s during the Iran-Iraq war to bypass Hormuz.
It connects eastern oilfields to the Red Sea port of Yanbu and is operating at its expanded seven million barrels per day capacity.
Aramco uses about two million bpd domestically, leaving roughly 5 million bpd for export. Yanbu loadings averaged a near-capacity 4.6 million bpd in the week starting March 23, shipping data shows, despite attacks targeting the hub on March 19.
Overall Saudi crude exports fell 26 per cent year-on-year in March to 4.39 million bpd, Kpler and JODI data showed.
Still, higher prices increased the value of those exports by roughly US$558 million from a year earlier. Riyadh had pre-emptively boosted exports in February to their highest since April 2023.
Despite the advantage of the East-West link, Quilliam said Saudi Arabia remained vulnerable to further strikes by Iran or its allies in Yemen, the Houthis, against its energy infrastructure in the west and vessels passing through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait to the Red Sea.
The UAE has been shielded to an extent by its 1.5 to 1.8 million bpd Habshan-Fujairah pipeline, which bypasses the strait. Its estimated oil export value still fell by more than US$174 million year-on-year in March.
Among the Gulf producers, Iraq's revenue fell the most - plunging by 76 per cent to US$1.73 billion. Kuwait was next with a 73 per cent fall to US$864 million. Iraq state oil marketer SOMO said on April 2 that March oil revenues were about US$2 billion, close to Reuters' estimate.
Adriana Alvarado, vice-president of sovereign ratings at Morningstar DBRS, said Gulf governments had options to shore up their finances and could either draw on fiscal savings or go to the financial markets to issue debt.
"Apart from Bahrain, the Gulf states have enough fiscal room to deal with the shock, with government debt at moderate levels below 45 per cent of GDP," she added.
For the longer term, the impact is unclear. Some oil companies and politicians in the West have lobbied for increased investment in fossil fuels to try to protect against supply shocks.
In an early indicator of how the crisis could accelerate a shift from reliance on oil, last week France's TotalEnergies and UAE state-backed renewable energy firm Masdar announced a US$2.2 billion joint venture to rapidly deploy renewable energy across nine Asian countries.
The writers are from Reuters
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
